title: Recognizing Groups in Erdős Geometry and Model Theory
speaker: Artem Chernikov
abstract: Erdős-style geometry is concerned with combinatorial questions about simple geometric objects, such as counting incidences between finite sets of points, lines, etc. These questions can be typically viewed as asking for the possible number of intersections of a given (semi-)algebraic variety with large finite grids of points.

proof_stages.label: Classical expansion prototypes
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the sum-product phenomenon and the bivariate polynomial expansion theorem, emphasizing that finite sets expand unless the map is built from purely additive or multiplicative one-variable pieces.

proof_stages.label: Elekes–Szabó surface dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: For an algebraic surface with finite-to-one coordinate projections, the prototype dichotomy is stated: either every finite grid has a power-saving intersection bound, or locally the relation becomes the ternary graph of addition after analytic coordinate changes.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional o-minimal theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker passes to arity r and coordinate dimension m, introduces grids in general position, and states the new theorem for relations definable in o-minimal expansions: explicit power saving in general position or local correspondence with an m-dimensional abelian real group.

proof_stages.label: Non-expansion limit geometry
proof_stages.summary: When power saving fails along growing grids, ultraproducts of those grids and algebraic closure in a nonstandard field produce a pregeometry/matroid whose modularity is the model-theoretic signal that a group should be recoverable.

proof_stages.label: Group-configuration reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: The group-configuration ingredient is explained through a combinatorial baby theorem: a high-arity relation satisfying the uniqueness and rectangularity properties of an abelian sum relation determines an abelian group, with the actual proof using generic finite correspondences.

proof_stages.label: Incidence bounds for quantitative power saving
proof_stages.summary: Definable incidence bounds, especially improvements for K_{2,2}-free bipartite relations over the general Zarankiewicz exponent, supply the quantitative delta that is converted into the theorem's explicit gamma.

proof_stages.label: Definable group structure and scope
proof_stages.summary: The recovered model-theoretic group is interpreted using the structure theory of definable groups in o-minimal settings, yielding the local real abelian group conclusion while clarifying the role of general position, possible nonabelian extensions, and complexity dependence.

examples.name: Arithmetic progressions in an additive group
examples.note: Arithmetic progressions illustrate the converse direction of the Elekes–Szabó alternative: a locally additive relation can attain quadratic grid intersections, so power saving cannot hold.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only says one can use arithmetic progressions in the abelian group to get quadratic intersections, without carrying out the construction.

passes.reason: Arithmetic progressions are highly regular additive sets witnessing the tight maximal grid-intersection rate, not a refutation.
Arithmetic progressions are highly regular tight additive configurations used to realize n^2 intersections, showing power saving fails in the addition-like case.
Arithmetic progressions are maximally additively regular/minimal-doubling subsets used to produce the full n^2 intersections, obstructing universal power saving.
Arithmetic progressions are maximally regular small-doubling subsets used here to attain the maximal n^2 intersections, not to refute the stated dichotomy.
equally spaced additive progressions are highly structured small-doubling sets used to attain maximal grid intersections and obstruct universal power saving
